brief_summary: A vertical price ladder with three tiers mapped against a price axis ($80 at the bottom, $250 at the top). Bottom tier: budget HE $80–$120, annotated with 'configurator shallow or desktop-only, calibration manual or absent, magnet tolerance loose' — rendered in a muted stroke. Middle tier: mid HE $130–$160, annotated with 'browser configurator, per-key rapid trigger, reliable calibration' — rendered with amber accent. Top tier: enthusiast HE $160–$250, annotated with 'firmware parity with hardware, tight magnet tolerance, hot-swap open standard' — rendered with amber accent. An amber bracket spans the top two tiers with the label 'where the firmware matches the hardware'. The diagram directly supports the article's pricing-reality section claims.
data_sources: hall-effect-keyboard-guide.mdx § 'The pricing reality' — 'Budget Hall-effect boards in the $80–$120 range exist and they are real keyboards. They cut corners on configurator depth — often a desktop-only app, often no per-key actuation control — or on calibration quality.'

hall-effect-keyboard-guide.mdx § 'The pricing reality' — 'The enthusiast tier at $150–$250 is where the firmware typically matches the hardware. Per-key rapid trigger is exposed in a browser configurator, calibration is reliable, and the magnet tolerance is tight enough that WASD behavior is consistent across the cluster.'
